title.rendered: City throws support behind Paint Branch Chinese immersion program

excerpt.rendered: The College Park City Council voted Tuesday to send a letter to the Prince George’s County Board of Education (BOE) in support of continued funding for the Chinese immersion program at Paint Branch Elementary School. The letter came two days before the board is scheduled to vote on its proposed fiscal 2027 budget and follows […]

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Shawn Joseph, Interim Superintendent of Schools for Prince George’s County Public Schools
Judith White, Prince George’s County Public Schools Chief Academic Officer
Alexandra Tyukavina, Paint Branch PTA Secretary
organizations: Prince George’s County Board of Education
extraction.ai_summary: The College Park City Council voted to send a letter to the Prince George’s County Board of Education in support of continued funding for the Chinese immersion program at Paint Branch Elementary School. The letter, signed by Mayor Fazlul Kabir, asks the board to fund the full program for all Paint Branch students, stating that “The program has become an important part of the broader educational ecosystem serving College Park residents” and that “Changes that significantly reduce immersion opportunities could undermine years of investment by educators, students and the community.” The council’s letter comes as the board is scheduled to vote on its proposed fiscal 2027 budget, which includes a plan to limit enrollment to 25 students in each grade from kindergarten through fifth grade.

excerpt.rendered: The College Park City Council on Feb. 3 unanimously approved an ordinance that will prevent landlords from asking tenants to renew a lease sooner than 180 days from the end of the current lease. The so-called early lease ordinance, overwhelmingly supported by students renting apartments in high-rise buildings on or near Route 1, was introduced […]

key_people: Sami Saeed, University of Maryland Public Policy Major
Holly Simmons, College Park Councilmember
Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
David Dorsch, College Park Resident and Landlord
Richard Biffl, Landlord
Nick DiSpirito, University of Maryland Student Liaison to the College Park City Council
extraction.ai_summary: The College Park City Council unanimously approved an ordinance preventing landlords from asking tenants to renew a lease sooner than 180 days from the end of the current lease. The ordinance was introduced in September and supported by students renting apartments in high-rise buildings, who shared stories of landlords approaching them as soon as a month after move-in with threats of rent increases or replacement. Sami Saeed, a University of Maryland public policy major, said 'The stress that that caused me … trying to find anyone to live with for next year. I had to call 50 people. … That is the situation renters are faced with. That is the situation I was faced with. It happened to me.'

title.rendered: Committee pushes for changes at school bus stops

excerpt.rendered: A citizen-led committee resubmitted a year-old report on school bus stop safety to the College Park City Council in January in an effort to encourage officials to make changes to protect elementary school children. College Park’s Bicycle and Pedestrian Advisory Committee (BPAC) in October 2024 recommended that the city upgrade safety at seven school bus […]

key_people: Donald Hays, College Park Bicycle and Pedestrian Advisory Committee Subcommittee Chairman
Jacob Hernandez, College Park City Councilmember
Alan Hew, College Park City Councilmember
organizations: College Park Bicycle and Pedestrian Advisory Committee
extraction.ai_summary: A citizen-led committee resubmitted a year-old report on school bus stop safety to the College Park City Council, recommending upgrades at seven bus stops with low safety scores. Donald Hays, the chairman of the BPAC subcommittee, stated 'I personally feel that is an outrage and there has been very little movement and very little progress' regarding the implementation of the recommendations. The committee's requests include adding bike lanes and sidewalks to protect elementary school children, with some council members, such as Jacob Hernandez and Alan Hew, supporting the additions.

title.rendered: Lakeland to add artwork highlighting its history

excerpt.rendered: Residents of College Park’s Lakeland neighborhood are planning to add 10 murals to sidewalks, storm drains, fences, retaining walls and paths throughout the community. The project is a continuation of a decades-long effort to highlight the history of Lakeland, said Maxine Gross, a member of the Lakeland Community Heritage Project and a fifth-generation Lakeland resident. […]

key_people: Maxine Gross, Lakeland Community Heritage Project Member
Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
extraction.ai_summary: The Lakeland neighborhood in College Park is planning to add 10 murals to highlight its history, as part of a continuation of a decades-long effort. According to Maxine Gross, a member of the Lakeland Community Heritage Project, 'It's just a way for folks to be able to see the community that we have been talking about for a number of years.' The project has a total budget of $28,382, with $12,650 coming from a 2022 grant from the Maryland Heritage Areas Authority and $15,732 from city funds, and will be a joint effort among city officials, community members, and University of Maryland partners.

title.rendered: Students suggest ways to ease neighborhood tensions near UMD

excerpt.rendered: Three housing policy students in December suggested that the city and the University of Maryland (UMD) could do more to help undergraduate tenants become better neighbors to the long-term homeowners who live near them. The students, part of the UMD Partnership for Action Learning in Sustainability Program, told the College Park City Council that students […]

key_people: Frances Chen, University of Maryland Student
Nicholas Crane, University of Maryland Student
Ashna Pandya, University of Maryland Student
Alan Hew, College Park City Councilmember
extraction.ai_summary: Three University of Maryland students, Frances Chen, Nicholas Crane, and Ashna Pandya, presented to the College Park City Council on ways to ease neighborhood tensions near the university. Chen stated that the university campus can only accommodate about 20% of its current 30,000 student population, leading to what she called 'studentification' of established neighborhoods, which can degrade a community's quality of life. Councilmember Alan Hew suggested that the university should take more responsibility in teaching its students how to be good neighbors, stating 'The university well knows that, after two years living on campus, students are expected to live off campus... They have not taken any responsibility in preparing the students living off campus.'

excerpt.rendered: College Park City Council members in January asked city staff to conduct a study to determine whether to restrict parking in residential neighborhoods by issuing permits. Some neighborhoods, including those with crowded streets and few driveways in North College Park and around the University of Maryland campus, already have permit-only parking. The study would examine […]

key_people: Jim Miller, Parking Enforcement Officer
Susan Whitney, College Park Councilmember
extraction.ai_summary: The College Park City Council is considering expanding permit parking in residential neighborhoods, with Parking Enforcement Officer Jim Miller proposing a study to examine the issue. Miller suggested that permit-only parking could help address overcrowding in homes by only allowing homeowners and tenants whose names appear on a lease to qualify for permits, and proposed charging an annual fee for each permit, saying 'Where are they going to park their car now, because they are not on a lease agreement, they are not listed as [the] owner-occupied property owner... Without providing the necessary documentation that we require, they won’t be able to get a permit, so they’ll have to figure out where their car goes.' Councilmembers had mixed reactions to the proposal, with former Councilmember Susan Whitney suggesting that paid street parking could lead to less green space and increased stormwater runoff, and instead proposing incentives such as tax breaks to encourage residents to own fewer cars, saying 'We want people who need their cars to be able to park them, and we want people who can get by with one car to do that and to be encouraged to do that'.

title.rendered: Affordable senior housing to open on Branchville Road

excerpt.rendered: The College Park City Council agreed Jan. 27 to recommend that Prince George’s County amend a parking ordinance that would allow a developer to build a four-story, 87-unit apartment building for older residents on Branchville Road. Development of the 2.2-acre site at 4810 Branchville Road has been approved twice before: in 1987 for a 96-unit […]

key_people: Matthew Tedesco, Lawyer for Cruz Development Corp.
Kelly Jordan, College Park City Councilmember
Carlos Nunes, Senior Planner for the City of College Park
extraction.ai_summary: The College Park City Council agreed to recommend amending a parking ordinance to allow a developer to build an 87-unit affordable senior housing apartment building on Branchville Road. According to Matthew Tedesco, the project "emphasizes aging in place" and will include elevators, social spaces, and amenities for senior citizens. As Councilmember Kelly Jordan noted, "There is a real need for senior housing in College Park," and the developer will use a 4% federal low-income housing tax credit to finance the project, with rents based on 60% of the area median income.

title.rendered: Governor pushes for smaller, denser housing

excerpt.rendered: Maryland Gov. Wes Moore has proposed a drastic change in Maryland zoning law to promote construction of more affordable homes, but state officials who represent College Park are divided on whether it would be a good thing. Moore has introduced a bill in the Maryland General Assembly—SB 36 in the Senate and HB 239 in […]

key_people: Wes Moore, Maryland Governor
Joseline Peña-Melnyk, House Speaker, Maryland House of Delegates
Jim Rosapepe, State Senator, Maryland Senate
Tom Dernoga, Prince George’s County Councilmember
extraction.ai_summary: Maryland Governor Wes Moore has proposed a bill to change Maryland zoning law, allowing for more affordable homes by reducing the required property size per single-family house to 5,000 square feet, which would enable at least eight houses per acre. House Speaker Joseline Peña-Melnyk supports the proposal, stating 'Maryland has a housing shortage of roughly 100,000 homes... When people can’t afford to live here, we lose workers. We lose families. And we lose the economic growth that allows us to fund schools, transportation and public safety.' However, State Senator Jim Rosapepe and Prince George’s County Councilmember Tom Dernoga have expressed concerns or opposition to the proposal, with Dernoga calling it a 'sprawl multiplier which will worsen congestion, pollution, school overcrowding and will strain public safety resources.'

excerpt.rendered: College Park City Councilmember Alan Hew (District 1) doesn’t wear a badge or carry a gun, but he knows an awful lot about the police. That’s because he has graduated from three different citizen police academies, where he learned how officers do their jobs and create relationships with members of the community. “There’s a lot […]

key_people: Alan Hew, College Park City Councilmember
Erica Johnson, Prince George's County Police Department Sergeant
Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
extraction.ai_summary: College Park City Councilmember Alan Hew has graduated from three different citizen police academies, learning about police operations and creating relationships with community members. Hew stated, 'There's a lot of speculation of how they operate and I wanted to be able to speak confidently in support of the departments that operate within our city.' His training has helped him contribute to council discussions about safety and understand police operations, which he can share with his constituents, and according to Fazlul Kabir, 'it is important' for building trust and understanding between the community and police.

title.rendered: Winter storm named Fern continues to hit Route 1 corridor with snow, sleet and freezing rain

excerpt.rendered: A dangerous winter storm continued to bring a mix of snow, sleet and freezing rain across the metro region Sunday, as local jurisdictions mounted snow removal and emergency response efforts. In Laurel, College Park and Hyattsville, as well as much of Prince George’s County, the forecast called for heavy sleet accumulation Sunday afternoon, with as […]

key_people: Aisha Braveboy, Prince George’s County Executive
Keith R. Sydnor, Laurel Mayor
Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
organizations: City of Laurel Public Works Department
extraction.ai_summary: A dangerous winter storm named Fern brought snow, sleet, and freezing rain to the Route 1 corridor, prompting local jurisdictions to mount snow removal and emergency response efforts. Prince George’s County Executive Aisha Braveboy stated, 'The county’s snow response operation is fully active. Crews are working around the clock to clear and treat the roadways and public safety remains are top priority.' College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir reminded residents to report icy sidewalks and unsafe conditions to the city and to check when the streets they live on were last plowed through an interactive map.
